bids.reasoning: It is the very beginning of the negotiation (t=0.0), and the opponent's starting offer yields minimal utility for me across all issues. To establish a strong anchor and protect my high-value issues, I will counter with my optimal offer. There is no need to make concessions this early in the process.

The negotiation is in its very early stages (t=0.02). The opponent has made a tiny concession on issueA, shifting from valueF to valueE. To reciprocate, show flexibility, and explore their preferences, I will concede one step on my least important issue (issueD), changing it from valueA to valueB, while holding firm on my most highly valued issues.

The negotiation is still extremely early (t=0.04). The opponent has made small concessions on issueA, moving from valueF to valueD, so I will continue to hold firm on my highest priority issues (issueA and issueB). To reciprocate their movement, I will make a minor concession on issueC while holding my slight concession on issueD from the last round, keeping my utility well above 0.95.

The opponent is steadily conceding on issueA, which is my highest priority (0.4 importance), while remaining firm on the other issues. This suggests they care less about issueA and more about the others. Since it is still early in the negotiation (t=0.06), I will hold my ground on my most important issues (issueA and issueB) and offer a small concession on my lowest priority issue (issueD) to signal a willingness to compromise and explore potential tradeoffs.

The opponent is conceding rapidly on `issueA` (moving from valueF to valueB), which happens to be my highest-weight issue, while holding completely firm on `issueB`, `issueC`, and `issueD`. This suggests they likely value issues B, C, and D more than A. Since it is still extremely early in the negotiation (t=0.08), I will maintain my top-utility values for `issueA` and `issueB` (my most important issues) and continue to gradually concede on `issueC` and `issueD` to signal a willingness to compromise on issues that are less important to me but potentially more important to them.

The opponent has made full concessions on issueA (conceding to valueA), which holds my highest utility weight, but they remain anchored at valueF for issues B, C, and D. Since the time frame is still early (t=0.1), I will secure valueA for issueA and make a significant concession on my lowest-priority issue, issueD, by giving them valueF. This signals cooperation and targets a high utility of 0.856 for myself.

The opponent has fully conceded on issueA to my top value (valueA), indicating they might weight it lower, and they have just started conceding on issueB. Since time is still very early (t=0.12), I will lock in issueA at valueA and start offering modest concessions on my second most important issue (issueB) by moving it from valueA to valueB, while holding issueC at valueC and keeping issueD at valueF to map their preferences.

The negotiation is still in its early stages (t=0.14) and the opponent is making steady concessions, having fully conceded on my most important issue (issueA) and starting to concede on my second most important issue (issueB). I will accept their concession on issueA and hold firm on issueB to encourage further movement, while making a slight concession on issueC to offer something in return. I will keep my least important issue (issueD) at the value they strongly prefer (valueF) to maximize their gains at low cost to myself.

The negotiation is still in its early stages (t=0.16). The opponent has fully conceded on issueA to my highest preference (valueA) and is progressively conceding on issueB (now at valueC). To reciprocate their ongoing concessions and build momentum towards an agreement, I will continue to hold firm on issueA and issueB (valueB), while making a small concession on issueC (from valueD to valueE). I am leaving issueD at valueF since it is of lowest importance to me and I have already conceded on it.

The opponent has been conceding systematically on issues A and B, recently offering `valueA` for `issueA` and `valueB` for `issueB`. My utility for their last offer is 0.712. Since it is still very early in the negotiation (t=0.18), I will hold my ground on my last offer to see if they are willing to concede slightly on `issueC` before I accept their terms on the lesser issues.

The opponent previously conceded to 'valueB' for issueB, but in their latest offer they slid backward to 'valueC' while matching my ask of 'valueE' for issueC. I am retracting my compromise on issueC (moving back to 'valueD') to signal that backsliding on agreed values is unacceptable while maintaining their preferred 'valueF' for issueD and 'valueA' for issueA. This keeps my utility strong at 0.776 while demonstrating flexibility in upcoming offers.

To test if they prefer getting their way entirely on issueC and issueD, I will offer them `valueF` on both, but ask for `valueA` on issueB in return.
